label: Class · The Elephant in the Room
subtitle: AI, smart objects & your career — what to actually believe
verdict: The deferred AI talk, finally given. Frames AI in the lineage that started week 1 — not hype, not doom. Strong opening; the “interview script” section landed best. Pre-filled from slide deck — no transcript captured.

sections.label: Why this matters for your career
sections.summary: Everyone in your interviews will say “AI-first.” You'll say “Xerox PARC built this in 1973.” The hype gets you in the door; the history makes you the one they remember.

sections.label: Interview script · the full answer
sections.summary: A reusable answer for the inevitable “what do you think about AI?” interview question. Practiced in pairs.

sections.label: AI limitation audit
sections.summary: Walk through three real product failures where AI was loud but the design was quiet wrong. Affordances first, models second.

sections.label: Editing · before / after examples
sections.summary: Side-by-side AI-edited vs human-edited interaction copy. The class voted on which read better — and why.

sections.label: Prompt architecture
sections.summary: How a prompt is a UI for the model. System prompt = product voice. User prompt = customer input. Output schema = data contract.

sections.label: Norman's vocabulary applied to AI
sections.summary: Affordances, signifiers, mappings, feedback — what each means when the “object” is a model.

sections.label: Where AI falls on the calm/loud spectrum
sections.summary: Most AI products are loud by default. Calm-tech principles applied: only as much attention as the moment deserves.

sections.label: The attention problem
sections.summary: AI is the latest in a long line of attention-extracting designs. Where do we draw the line? Class-wide discussion ran long.

sections.label: Two visions of AI
sections.summary: Agent (does things for you) vs material (something you compose with). Most students felt the second framing was new.

sections.label: Smart objects + AI · our design challenge
sections.summary: What's the smart-objects move under AI? Implicit context for the model; explicit handles for the person.

sections.label: That's why we build
sections.summary: Closing: build the thing, then critique it. Models change quarterly; principles stay.

assignments.title: Write your own interview script
assignments.spec: Two paragraphs. The first answers “what do you think about AI?” in your voice. The second cites one historical reference and one current example.
rubric: Names the affordance you care about.

assignments.title: Audit one product
assignments.spec: Pick a real AI feature in a shipping product. Apply Norman's vocabulary. Where are the affordances strong? Where do they fail? 250 words max.
rubric: Names a specific feature, not the whole product.
Identifies signifier, feedback, mapping, failure mode.

assignments.title: Prompt-as-UI sketch
assignments.spec: Sketch one of your project's prompts as a piece of UI. System prompt as the product voice; user prompt as the input field; output as a card.
rubric: Identifies what changes if the user is wrong, vs the model is wrong.

grammar.issue: The attention discussion ran 8 minutes over and ate the closing.
grammar.fix: Cap at 12 min; surface the longer version as a Slack thread.

ideas.title: AI-as-material
ideas.summary: Treat the model as a material — like ink or thread — that you compose with. Reframes most of our project briefs.

ideas.title: The interview answer
ideas.summary: A one-page reusable answer for AI questions in interviews — class wanted this canonised on the wiki.

ideas.title: Calm prompt ladder
ideas.summary: Tiered prompts: ambient (no response), notice (one-line), conversation (full). Maps to Norman's spectrum.

engagement.label: Hands during discussion
engagement.note: More questions than any session this term.

engagement.label: Pair script practice
engagement.note: All pairs produced an interview answer in 8 min.

actions.title: Canonise the interview answer on the wiki
Cap attention discussion at 12 min next time
Open a Slack thread for the longer attention argument

quotes.speaker: Brett
quotes.text: Everyone will say AI-first. You'll say Xerox PARC built this in 1973. That's how you stand out.

quotes.speaker: Brett
quotes.text: The hype gets you in the door. The history makes you the one they remember.

quotes.speaker: Don Norman
quotes.text: Affordances exist whether or not they are visible. When they are visible, they are signifiers.

quotes.speaker: Class
quotes.text: AI is a material, not an agent — that's the move.
